3 2002 2001 2000 1999 1998 1997 1996 1995 1994 1993 1992 1991 1990 1989 1988 1987 1986 1985 1984 Skip to sidebar Interoperation of Heterogeneous CAD Tools in Ptolemy II by J. Liu, B. Wu, X. Liu, and E. A. Lee Symposium on Design, Test, and Microfabri

. A. Lee Symposium on Design, Test, and Microfabrication of MEMS/MOEMS (DTM'99) March 1999, Paris, France Prepublished version Published version ABSTRACT Typical complex systems that involve microsensors and microactuators exhibit heterogeneity both

ors and microactuators exhibit heterogeneity both at the implementation level and the problem level. For example, a system can be modeled using discrete events for digital circuits and SPICE-like analog descriptions for sensors. This heterogeneity ex

og descriptions for sensors. This heterogeneity exists not only in different implementation domains, but also at different levels of abstraction. This naturally leads to a heterogeneous approach to system design that uses domain-specific models of co

stem design that uses domain-specific models of computation (MoC) at various levels of abstractions to define a system, and leverages multiple CAD tools to do simulation, verification and synthesis. As the size and scope of the system increase, the i

s the size and scope of the system increase, the integration becomes too difficult and unmanageable if different tools are coordinated using simple scripts. In addition, for MEMS devices and mixed-signal circuits, it is essential to integrate tools w

nal circuits, it is essential to integrate tools with different MoC to simulate the whole system. Ptolemy II, a heterogeneous system-level design tool, supports the interaction among different MoCs. This paper discusses heterogeneous CAD tool interop

his paper discusses heterogeneous CAD tool interoperability in the Ptolemy II framework. The key is to understand the semantic interface and classify the tools by their MoC and their level of abstraction. Interfaces are designed for each domain so th

ion. Interfaces are designed for each domain so that the external tools can be easily wrapped. Then the interoperability of the tools becomes the interoperability of the semantics. Ptolemy II can act as the standard interface among different tools to
as the standard interface among different tools to achieve the overall design modeling. A micro-accelerometer with digital feedback isstudied as an example.
